raw_text.question: A bar of iron, previously heated to 1200°C, is cooled in a large bath of water maintained at a constant temperature of 50°C. The bar cools by 200° in the first minute. How much longer will it take to cool a second 200°?
raw_text.response: Okay, I'm trying to understand if a bar of iron moves from 1,200 degrees to 1,000 degrees in one minute. Okay, so we have 1,200 to 1,000 Celsius in one minute. That's based on a bath of water large enough for its temperature not to change at 50 degrees. So how much time would it take from 1,000 degrees to 800? Well, I don't remember the equation for the rate of heat transfer. Let me Google that one second. Heat transfer time equation. Okay, conduction. Conduction is, let's see what Gemini said. So, heat transferred over time equals to thermal conductivity of the material K, which is constant, cross-sectional area A, which is constant, and the temperature difference t1 minus t2 okay so t1 minus t2 is 60 seconds 60 seconds times ka was equals to 200 degrees i mean it's the heat but it's a it's we're talking about the same amount of degrees so it's going to be 200. Well, now if I need 200 more degrees, it's going to be... It's a linear equation, so it's going to be 60 seconds again. So the solution is it will take it another minute. If it took it the first minute to go down the first 200 degrees Celsius, to take the second 200 degrees Celsius, it will take the same time, one minute.
